title: Min Stack
code: private readonly Stack<(int Value, int Min)> _stack = new();

mins.label: min stack — minSoFar at each depth

frames.caption: Empty Min Stack. We keep a value stack and a parallel min stack; the min stack’s top is always the current minimum.
frames.note: The naive GetMin scans all elements: O(n). To reach O(1), we store the running minimum WITH every element. The reviewer does this with one stack of (value, minSoFar) pairs; the two stacks drawn here are the value-half and the min-half of those same pairs. Space is O(n) for the n stored pairs; every operation is O(1).

frames.caption: Push(5): new min = 5 (stack empty).
frames.note: On push, the new minimum is Min(val, currentMin) — comparing the incoming value against whatever was already the smallest. We store that running min ALONGSIDE the value (the reviewer keeps a (value, minSoFar) pair; here it is the matching cell in the min stack). This is the whole trick: by remembering the min at each depth, GetMin never has to rescan, so every operation stays O(1).

frames.caption: Push the pair (value 5, min 5) — one cell on each stack. Top of min stack is now 5.
frames.note: The pushed min cell (5) becomes the new current minimum at this depth. Notice it can stay the SAME as the level below (e.g. pushing 7 keeps min 3) — the min only ever moves down, never up, while pushing.

frames.caption: Push(3): new min = Min(3, 5) = 3.
frames.note: On push, the new minimum is Min(val, currentMin) — comparing the incoming value against whatever was already the smallest. We store that running min ALONGSIDE the value (the reviewer keeps a (value, minSoFar) pair; here it is the matching cell in the min stack). This is the whole trick: by remembering the min at each depth, GetMin never has to rescan, so every operation stays O(1).

frames.caption: Push the pair (value 3, min 3) — one cell on each stack. Top of min stack is now 3.
frames.note: The pushed min cell (3) becomes the new current minimum at this depth. Notice it can stay the SAME as the level below (e.g. pushing 7 keeps min 3) — the min only ever moves down, never up, while pushing.

frames.caption: Push(7): new min = Min(7, 3) = 3.
frames.note: On push, the new minimum is Min(val, currentMin) — comparing the incoming value against whatever was already the smallest. We store that running min ALONGSIDE the value (the reviewer keeps a (value, minSoFar) pair; here it is the matching cell in the min stack). This is the whole trick: by remembering the min at each depth, GetMin never has to rescan, so every operation stays O(1).

frames.caption: Push the pair (value 7, min 3) — one cell on each stack. Top of min stack is now 3.
frames.note: The pushed min cell (3) becomes the new current minimum at this depth. Notice it can stay the SAME as the level below (e.g. pushing 7 keeps min 3) — the min only ever moves down, never up, while pushing.

frames.caption: Push(2): new min = Min(2, 3) = 2.
frames.note: On push, the new minimum is Min(val, currentMin) — comparing the incoming value against whatever was already the smallest. We store that running min ALONGSIDE the value (the reviewer keeps a (value, minSoFar) pair; here it is the matching cell in the min stack). This is the whole trick: by remembering the min at each depth, GetMin never has to rescan, so every operation stays O(1).

frames.caption: Push the pair (value 2, min 2) — one cell on each stack. Top of min stack is now 2.
frames.note: The pushed min cell (2) becomes the new current minimum at this depth. Notice it can stay the SAME as the level below (e.g. pushing 7 keeps min 3) — the min only ever moves down, never up, while pushing.

frames.caption: GetMin() → 2. Just read the top of the min stack — O(1), no scan.
frames.note: GetMin just reads the TOP of the min stack — no scan, no compare loop. The top cell holds the minimum of every value currently present, because each push recorded Min(val, previousMin). That is why it is O(1).

frames.caption: Pop(): about to discard the top pair (value 2, min 2) from both stacks.
frames.note: Pop drops the top of the paired stack. The cell being removed (min 2) is the current global minimum — the test is whether the structure can recover the PREVIOUS min without rescanning.

frames.caption: Popped. Both stacks shrink by one; the min stack’s new top (3) is the restored minimum.
frames.note: Pop removes the top of BOTH stacks together (one (value, min) pair). Because each cell carried its own minSoFar, discarding the top instantly re-exposes the previous min underneath — no recomputation needed. Here popping (7, min 3) re-exposes (2, min 2)’s neighbor... actually the new top is (7,3)’s predecessor, restoring min 3 for free.

frames.caption: GetMin() → 3. Popping the global min (2) instantly restored the prior min (3) — no rescan.
frames.note: This is the payoff from the reviewer’s trace: carrying minSoFar in each cell means removing the smallest value re-exposes the next-smallest in O(1). GetMin tracks the minimum of the elements CURRENTLY present — it is not a sort, just an exact running minimum that survives pops.
